turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor the opinion of the Court this morning in case 23-1007, Cunningham versus Cornell University.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Regrettably for most of you who are here today, this won’t be the most engaging of cases. Section 1106 of the Employment Retirement Income Security Act, now you know why, better known as ERISA, prohibits fiduciaries who manage retirement plans from causing the plan to enter into certain transactions with parties and interests. A separate provision, Section 1108(b)(2)(A) exempts from 1106 prohibitions any transaction that involves contracting or making reasonable arrangements with a party in interest for services necessary for the establishment or operation of the plan if no more than reasonable compensation is paid thereof. The petitioners in this case represent the class of current and former Cornell University employees who participated in ERISA retirement plans from 2010 to 2016. In 2017, they sued responding Cornell and other planned fiduciaries for alleging causing the plan to engage in prohibited transactions for record keeping services in violation of 1106. The District Court dismissed this claim and the Second Circuit affirmed, but a Court of Appeal’s reason that 1108(b)(2)(A) exemption is incorporated into 1106’s prohibition, requiring plaintiffs to plea that a transaction prohibited under 1106 was also unnecessary or involved unreasonable compensation to survive a motion to dismiss. The Court disagrees and therefore reverses the decision below. Section 1108 sets out affirmative defenses so it is defendant fiduciaries who bear the burden to plead and prove that an exemption applies. Section 1106(a)(1)(C) contains just three elements: It prohibits fiduciaries from causing a plan to engage in a transaction; two, that the fiduciary knows or should know constitutes a direct or indirect furnishing of goods, services or facilities; and three, between the plan and a party in interest. Accordingly, to state a claim under 1106, a plaintiff need only plausibly allege those three elements. The exemptions to Section 1106’s prohibited transactions, which Congress placed in the separate statutory provision of its Section 1108, do not impose additional pleading requirements. This Court has previously held that when a statute has exemptions laid out apart from the prohibitions and the exemptions expressly referred to the prohibited conduct as such, the exemptions constitute affirmative defenses that are entirely responsible of the party raising them. Here Congress structured the 1108 exemptions in the orthodox format of an affirmative defense. Arisa’s structure confirms that 1108 exemptions or affirmative defenses, there are 21 exemptions enumerate in 1108 and the Secretary of Labor has promulgated hundreds of regulatory exemptions pursuant to Section 1108. Several of the exemptions turn on facts, one would expect to be in a fiduciary’s possession. It would therefore contravene basic notions of fairness to require plaintiffs to prove and disprove myriad exemptions before knowing which of them a defendant will seek to invoke. Respondents for their part raise serious concerns that future ERISA plaintiffs may bring meritless suits if they can state a claim by pleading only the elements contained within an 1106 provision. Those concerns however cannot overcome the statutory text and structure. Moreover, District Courts have various tools at their disposal to screen out meritless claims including requiring plaintiffs to file a reply to an answer addressing a claimed exemption under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 7(a). They can limit discovery, impose Rule 11 sanctions, and order cost shifting under ERISA. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the Court reverses the judgement of the Second Circuit and remands for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The decision of the Court is unanimous. Justice Alito has filed a concurring opinion in which Justices Thomas and Kavanaugh have joined.
